questions.questionText: Which of the following organisms is the most common cause of uncomplicated urinary tract infections (UTIs)?
questions.explanation: Uropathogenic E. coli (UPEC) causes >80% of community-acquired UTIs.

questions.questionText: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS), characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, is typically caused by:
questions.explanation: EHEC produces Shiga-like toxins that damage endothelial cells.

questions.questionText: Which organism causes typhoid fever and presents with 'rose spots' on the abdomen?
questions.explanation: S. Typhi causes systemic enteric fever and establishes a carrier state in the gallbladder.

questions.questionText: A non-lactose fermenting, oxidase-negative, highly motile gram-negative rod that produces H2S is most likely:
questions.explanation: Salmonella is motile and H2S positive; Shigella is non-motile and H2S negative.

questions.questionText: Vibrio cholerae causes massive watery 'rice-water' diarrhea through a toxin mechanism that:
questions.optionA: Inhibits protein synthesis by inactivating the 60S ribosome
questions.optionB: Permanently activates Gs, increasing intracellular cAMP
questions.optionC: Forms pores in the intestinal epithelial membrane
questions.explanation: Cholera toxin ADP-ribosylates the Gs alpha subunit, leading to fluid hypersecretion.

questions.questionText: An oxidase-positive, non-lactose fermenting gram-negative rod that produces a sweet, grape-like odor and green-blue pigment is:
questions.explanation: P. aeruginosa is notable for pyocyanin (blue) and pyoverdine (fluorescent green) pigments.

questions.questionText: Which organism is the major cause of gastritis and most peptic ulcers, and produces copious amounts of urease?
questions.explanation: H. pylori uses urease to create an alkaline microenvironment in the acidic stomach.

questions.questionText: A curved, 'seagull-shaped', microaerophilic gram-negative rod that is a leading cause of bacterial gastroenteritis in developed countries is:
questions.explanation: C. jejuni grows well at 42°C and is frequently acquired from undercooked poultry.

questions.questionText: Which bacteria demonstrates 'swarming' motility on an agar plate and produces urease, strongly predisposing to struvite kidney stones?
questions.explanation: Urease splits urea to ammonia, raising urine pH and precipitating Mg-ammonium-phosphate stones.

questions.optionB: A large polysaccharide capsule giving mucoid colonies and 'currant jelly' sputum
questions.optionC: A flagellum giving highly active swarming motility
questions.optionD: Absolute requirement for Factor X and V to grow
questions.explanation: The prominent capsule prevents phagocytosis and causes explosive, necrotizing lobar pneumonia.

questions.questionText: In Gram Negative Bacilli, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Microbiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Microbiology references for Gram Negative Bacilli. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Gram Negative Bacilli.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Gram Negative Bacilli scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Gram Negative Bacilli.
